ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Burmese pickled tea leaf salad (lahpet thoke). The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is the generic 'salad food product' class. A more specific term is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific code for lahpet thoke. A0CKM (composite salad dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Burmese-specific fermented tea leaf salad is not enumerated in the current FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for lahpet thoke or Burmese tea leaf salad as of 2026. SNOMED CT does not enumerate this dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Tea Leaf Salad in Singapore is approximately 150g, served as a side dish or light meal, presented in a small bowl or plate with all components tossed together.

nutrient_source.title: Tea Leaf Salad (Lahpet Thoke) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tea Leaf Salad (Lahpet Thoke), cr_id G00010604, with confidence 0.95. All per_100g macro and micronutrient values are taken from this source. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 6.5g/100g based on the fried component profile (peanut oil, palm oil used in frying garlic, broad beans, chickpeas) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; this is documented as an adapted estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on fermented/pickled vegetable salad with legume components
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lahpet thoke (Burmese tea leaf salad) in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2026. GI is estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on the following analogous evidence: (1) fermented/pickled vegetables have GI values in the range 20–35; (2) legume components (broad beans, chickpeas) have GI values of 35–40 per University of Sydney GI Database; (3) the dominant macronutrient is fat (44.5g/100g) which strongly suppresses glycemic response; (4) total carbohydrate is only 5.9g/100g. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (32 × 5.9 × 1.5) / 100 = 2.8g, classified as very low GL. This is a conservative analogous estimate and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810mg/100g — exceeds the 600mg/100g high-concern threshold; driven by fish sauce, dried shrimp, and fermented tea leaves (laphet). Potassium at 64mg/100g is very low, providing no meaningful sodium offset.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Tea Leaf Salad due to its very high sodium content of 810mg/100g; if consumed, request reduced fish sauce, substitute lime juice for seasoning, and limit portion to no more than 50g per occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is low at 5.9g/100g with sugar at 4.69g/100g; however, the very high fat content (44.54g/100g) raises concern for insulin resistance and cardiovascular co-morbidity in type 2 diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Tea Leaf Salad has a low glycemic load (GL ~2.8 per 150g serving) and low carbohydrate content, making it acceptable for blood glucose control in type 2 diabetes, but the high total fat and estimated saturated fat (~6.5g/100g) from fried components should be monitored in the context of overall daily fat intake and cardiovascular risk.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat is 44.54g/100g with estimated saturated fat ~6.5g/100g from fried garlic, peanuts, broad beans, and chickpeas; sodium at 810mg/100g further elevates cardiovascular risk. Cholesterol is 26mg/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit Tea Leaf Salad due to its high total fat (44.54g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~6.5g/100g), and very high sodium (810mg/100g); if consumed, portion should be restricted to 50–75g and preparation should use reduced fried components and minimal fish sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~6.5g/100g exceeds the 5g/100g RA threshold; sodium at 810mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue swelling; nightshade ingredients (tomato, chili) may trigger individual RA flares; deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Tea Leaf Salad due to high saturated fat, high sodium, nightshade ingredients, and AGE-generating fried components; the fermented tea leaf (laphet) component has polyphenol anti-inflammatory properties but these are outweighed by the pro-inflammatory fried additions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tea Leaf Salad, known in Burmese as Lahpet Thoke (လက်ဖက်သုတ်), is a traditional Burmese salad made from fermented or pickled tea leaves (laphet) tossed with an array of crunchy fried and roasted components including fried garlic, fried peanuts, fried broad beans, fried chickpeas, sesame seeds, dried shrimp, tomato, chili, and cabbage, dressed with fish sauce and lime juice. It is one of Myanmar's most iconic dishes and holds deep cultural significance, often served at ceremonial occasions, as a snack, or as a side dish. In Singapore, it is available at Burmese food stalls and is consumed as a breakfast item or teatime snack. The texture is a complex interplay of soft fermented tea leaves, crunchy fried legumes and nuts, and fresh vegetables. The flavour profile is umami-rich, salty, slightly sour, and nutty. Nutritionally, Tea Leaf Salad is calorie-dense at 430 kcal/100g, dominated by fat (44.54g/100g) from the fried components and peanuts, with low carbohydrate (5.9g/100g) and modest protein (1.32g/100g). Sodium is very high at 810mg/100g due to fish sauce, dried shrimp, and the fermented tea leaves themselves. This makes it unsuitable for patients with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant modification. The glycemic load is very low (~2.8 per 150g serving), making it acceptable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes, though the high fat content warrants monitoring. The fermented tea leaf component provides polyphenols with potential anti-inflammatory properties, but these are partially offset by the pro-inflammatory effects of deep-fried components and high sodium.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010604) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is an adapted estimate based on known fried component profile as HPB does not separately report this value. GI is an analogous estimate as no direct measurement exists for this dish.
notes: 1. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0g/100g by HPB. This is unusual given the vegetable and legume content of the dish; actual fibre may be higher in practice. The HPB value is preserved as reported. This is a confirmed zero per the HPB source, not a naturally zero value — it likely reflects a measurement or reporting limitation. 2. saturated_fat_g (6.5g/100g) is an adapted estimate derived from the known fried component profile (peanut oil, palm oil used in frying garlic, broad beans, chickpeas) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific Tea Leaf Salad preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~32, low) based on fermented vegetable and legume GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for lahpet thoke. 5. The food_group in the input was listed as 'Beverages / Tea and related drinks' which is incorrect for a salad dish; corrected to 'Mixed / Salads and mixed vegetable dishes'. 6. is_halal flag is set to false as the dish contains dried shrimp and fish sauce which require halal certification verification; halal status cannot be confirmed without vendor-specific certification. 7. dietary_fibre_g confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a reported value, not naturally zero — actual fibre content from legumes and vegetables may be underreported.
